query: How do I avoid race conditions when multiple writers update the same record?
context: Implementing a multi-writer sync protocol for a shared resource.

candidates.text: Pessimistic locking: acquire an exclusive lock on the row before write, release after commit. Safe but reduces throughput under contention.
Optimistic concurrency control with version numbers: each write increments a version field; on commit, detect conflict by comparing expected vs current version.
Redis pub/sub is good for async messaging between services.
Use a CRDT for the field if commutativity holds; merges become deterministic without locks.
Postgres advisory locks are a lightweight alternative to row locks for application-level coordination.
Use exponential backoff on retry to avoid thundering herd.
GraphQL subscriptions push state changes to clients in real time.
For idempotent writes, supply a deterministic request-id so retries don't double-apply.
S3 supports conditional puts via If-None-Match for ETag-based coordination.
Snowflake IDs encode timestamp + worker; useful for distributed ordering.

query: What is the standard way to handle async errors in Node.js?
context: Refactoring a callback-heavy module to use async/await.

candidates.text: Wrap await calls in try/catch and propagate via custom Error subclasses.
Use process.on('unhandledRejection') as a last-resort safety net; log and exit.
Promise.allSettled returns per-promise outcomes — useful when partial failures are OK.
Bash trap is the shell-script equivalent.
In Express, errors must be passed to next(err) to reach the central error middleware.
setImmediate vs setTimeout — both schedule on the event loop but at different phases.
Streams have their own 'error' event that you must subscribe to or they crash the process.
npm audit lists known-vulnerable dependencies.
Top-level await is supported in ES modules since Node 14.8.
Domain module is deprecated; do not introduce it in new code.

query: Why is my prompt cache hit rate dropping in production?
context: Anthropic API calls now cost 3x what they did last week.

candidates.text: Prompt cache has a 5-minute TTL; long idle periods between calls invalidate cached prefixes.
Any change to system prompt, tool definitions, or chat-template kwargs invalidates the cached prefix.
Smaller models are usually cheaper but cache hit rate depends on call distribution, not model size.
Use Anthropic's cache_control: ephemeral marker on stable prefix blocks to force caching.
Memory leaks in long-lived Node processes show up as growing RSS over hours.
You can monitor cache effectiveness via the usage.cache_read_input_tokens field on each response.
Streaming responses can sometimes mask token counts; sample non-streamed for accurate accounting.
OpenTelemetry traces should include user_id and model_id labels for cost attribution.
Token bucket rate-limiters work well for burst-prone clients.
Prompt caching pricing: cache writes cost 1.25x base; cache reads cost 0.1x base.

query: Best way to commit a feature flag rollout in our repo?
context: Just finished implementing a new gated path; need to ship safely.

candidates.text: We use GrowthBook; flags live in growthbook.yaml at repo root.
Always ship the gate as one commit; ship the gated feature in a separate commit so revert is single-file.
Roll out at 1% → 10% → 50% → 100% with at least 30 minutes between bumps for metric stabilization.
Dark launches: deploy code but keep flag off until ops signs off.
Database migrations should run before the flag is enabled, not after.
Tailwind v4 is the latest major version.
For internal-only features, scope by user.is_employee instead of percentile rollout.
Always require an owner on each flag definition; flags older than 90 days without an owner get auto-flagged.
Type tests stay green even when runtime tests fail — don't trust tsc alone.
Statsig is an alternative to GrowthBook but we picked GrowthBook for self-hosting.

query: How should I test a Bun script that spawns a subprocess?
context: Writing a new harness; the subprocess opens a port and answers HTTP.

candidates.text: Bun's spawn returns a Subprocess; use child.exited to wait for termination.
For port collision tests, bind to an ephemeral port (0) and read the assigned one back from the listener.
Mock global fetch by reassigning globalThis.fetch in the test; restore in afterEach.
Vitest is the alternative to Bun's test runner; faster for some workloads.
Buffered stderr surface on boot fail is a pattern we use across managed-server lifecycle tests.
GitHub Actions runners have ~14 GB RAM but parallel workloads share it; serialize heavy tests with needs:.
Use AbortController to time-bound async test calls; throw on timeout.
Always teardown owned subprocesses in finally; otherwise leaked PIDs accumulate.
snapshot tests are flaky when the output includes wall-clock timestamps.
FFI via bun:ffi is faster than spawn but only for compiled native binaries.
